However, I can provide a detailed answer to your question regarding the effects of European industrialization.

The emergence of the factory system fundamentally reshaped European society and its economic structure. Socially, the massive movement of people from rural areas to burgeoning urban centers created dense, often desperate, working-class communities. The old feudal hierarchies eroded; the landed aristocracy's power lessened as the wealthy, entrepreneurial bourgeoisie gained immense economic leverage. A new class structure emerged: the proletariat, dependent entirely on wage labor, and the owners of capital.

Economically, industrialization ushered in the age of modern capitalism. Mass production, powered by steam and coal, replaced localized, agrarian economies. This intense drive for efficiency and profit spurred extensive global trade networks. Europe became the epicenter of industrial power, developing complex financial systems and establishing a relentless, competitive economic engine that defined the modern era.<turn|>
